feedback.text: # Abstract All video games sold/licensed to customers can work indefinitely, however, a new dark pattern has emerged, with which publishers take away video games from customers **after the sale**. Our survey has shown, that if it were up to publishers, 96% of video games with this dark pattern would be destroyed forever. I ask the Commission to take action. # Full letter Dear ladies and gentlemen, there is a significant need to address the remote disabling video games. All video games sold/licensed to customers can work indefinitely, however, a new dark pattern has emerged over the last ten to twelve years, where these video games are being taken away from the customer **after the sale**. This works by tethering a video game to a server, which the publisher controls. After a large number of sales has been made, the publisher shuts down the server, rendering every tethered video game sold/licensed worldwide dysfunctional immediately. The publishers deliberately withhold vital components from customers and by employing deliberate digital restrictions, make repair almost impossible. Publishers deprive the customers of their private property and keep the money from the sale. The only solution offered by publishers, if any, is to buy a new product, which has a similar dark pattern built in. I see this dark pattern as a form of planned obsolescence, which digitisation has enabled. Our Stop Destroying Videogames European Citizens Initiative has conducted a survey of 1.038 video games with this tethered dark pattern built in. You will find the file 2025-08-11 Tethered Dark Pattern Game List.xlsx attached. If it were left to the publishers alone, ** 96% of all tethered video games would be destroyed. ** Currently however, thanks to multi-year efforts of programmers, cryptography experts, network engineers and other IT-professionals, only 75% are being destroyed. 21% get preserved by multi-year repair efforts of various IT-professionals through reverse-engineering. This is outside of reasonable expectation of repair for customers. An analogy would be if customers were expected to build a new engine from scratch, for their car in their garage. This is such a monumental effort, because publishers withhold vital components or even documentation. The number of games destroyed will only grow, if the situation continues. The council has identified video games as an integral part of the ecosystem of cultural and creative industries and as a 23.48 billion Euro heavy industry in the Union (Source: 15901/23). The parliament recognises the need for additional consumer protection in the video games industry, which will ensure a safe and trustworthy online environment and that building confidence among consumers can boost the economic growth of the video game industry (Source: A9-0300/2022). Many groups currently urge De-Bureaucratisation, but as the EU has shown, through the new Battery Regulation for example, actions of lawmakers can foster and build entire economic sectors. Our Stop Destroying Videogames ECI has published a technical manual for video game publishers for preserving tethered video games (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qXy9GlKgrlM). It was created together with video games- and IT-professionals and contains multiple examples for preservation, technical recommendations when designing a new game and preserving an already published game. With this landmark regulation, the EU will ensure a trustworthy environment for consumers and European Video Games companies will have a competitive advantage, thanks to their games lasting forever; hence in the rest of the world other tethered video games can be destroyed at any moment without warning. Thus, I kindly ask the Commission to take action on this matter, not only in the interest of consumer protection, but in the interest of European competitiveness in the video games sector as well. With highest esteem, Sebastian Herndlhofer
